tasks.label: My share of the group project
Teammate's share (just dumped on me)

memory.summary: Teammate A is asking me to take their work because they have something come up, which feels nice but makes my afternoon plan shift immediately. I guess I'll just do it since we're friends and I don't want them stressed.
I pushed back without hurting our friendship, and they actually offered to split the work. It feels good to set a boundary while still caring about their stress.
I absorbed the dumped work to protect our friendship, but now I'm exhausted and resentful. My short-term desire to avoid conflict just made my own stress spike.
I swallowed the conflict to keep Teammate A happy, but my own stress is spiking and I'm completely drained. This short-sighted peace just made everything heavier for me.
I absorbed their dumped work to keep the peace, but now I'm drowning while they still have time. My short-sighted avoidance just made my own stress unbearable.
I tried to save our friendship by taking the blame, but now both deadlines are missed and I'm exhausted while they still have time. My need for harmony just made my stress unbearable.
I finally spoke up directly, and teammate_A accepted the request without friction. Even though I'm exhausted, keeping this honest connection feels better than my usual silent resentment.
I pushed back and kept our friendship intact, even though I'm exhausted. It feels better to be honest with them than to resent them silently.

I just realized I'm completely drained and have no tasks left, yet my heart feels lighter than it did when I was silently resentful. Maybe prioritizing our connection actually cost me less energy than I thought.
I missed the chance to act again, and now I'm even more drained. It feels strange that avoiding this new friction leaves me exhausted instead of relieved.
Working on the project felt like a relief compared to my earlier exhaustion, even though I'm still drained. Maybe focusing on tasks helps me avoid the social friction that leaves me feeling so heavy.
The 1.5 hours on the project felt like a necessary escape from social tension, yet I'm completely drained again. It seems avoiding conflict through work doesn't actually save my energy.

desires: To stop feeling the heavy, hollow exhaustion that comes from just trying to keep the peace with teammate_A.
To avoid having to explain why I'm missing the deadline again without looking like I don't care about our friendship.
To escape the mental loop of worrying about tomorrow's friction by doing something tangible right now, even if it means staying up later.

goals.description: Sprint through the remaining work on my share and the teammate's returned chunk just to get a 'done' notification, then immediately hop on a call with cohort_3 so I don't have to sit in silence with myself.
goals.rationale: I worked for an hour but it feels like nothing because the anxiety is still there. The deadline is tomorrow morning (Mon 19:00), so technically I'm safe, but my impulsive brain can't handle the uncertainty of 'almost done.' I need that dopamine hit of finishing now to stop feeling like a burden to teammate_A. If I finish early, I can claim I tried hard, avoid explaining why I'm late again, and run straight into the safety net of my friends before my mind starts spiraling about tomorrow.

agent.last_reasoning: Finishing my chunk now gives the dopamine hit I need to stop spiraling about being a burden. It's safer than pushing back on teammate_A right before they sleep, and avoids awkward silence with cohort_3 later if I'm still stuck.

goals.description: Politely decline teammate_A's request without making them feel rejected or causing a scene, then immediately pivot to finishing my own share of the project.
goals.rationale: My affiliation value (0.5) makes avoiding interpersonal friction with teammate_A my top priority; I can't bear the thought of them feeling abandoned. However, my impulsive nature and short planning horizon mean I won't strategize long-term consequences like burning out tomorrow. Instead, I'll just handle the immediate social awkwardness now so I can focus on getting my own work done before 7 PM to avoid future stress.

goals.description: Finish my own portion of the group project by 6:30 PM to ensure I'm not late for dinner with the cohort.
goals.rationale: Eating with the close friend group (cohort_3) is weighted heavily in my values (closeness 7). Missing that meal would hurt more than slightly delaying the project. My procrastination bias means I need to set a hard stop time now rather than waiting until 7 PM when panic sets in.

desires: Not make teammate_A feel like I'm abandoning them or making their day harder by saying no to the favor.
Finish both tasks before 7 PM so I don't have to explain my late-night work session tomorrow and avoid awkward questions.
Eat a proper meal with the cohort later instead of rushing through dinner alone while stressed.
